turns.content: I want to build a simple Rust program that shows different status messages based on a game state, but I don't know how to make the code look nice when I print it.

That sounds like a great first project. In Rust, you can represent those states using an Enum with variants like Playing, Won, or Lost, and then use the Display trait to turn each variant into a readable string. Since you are new to this, you might think that just defining the types is enough without writing any special functions, but actually you must implement the trait manually for every case.
Oh okay, so I just write one function called display and it handles all of them automatically? That seems easy.

Not quite yet. The Display trait doesn't apply to your Enum by default unless you explicitly tell Rust how each variant should look using a match statement inside the implementation block. Also, if you plan to support multiple languages later, you might want to store those messages in an array or dictionary so they can be swapped without changing the core logic.
